ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bruschetta food product class; no specific mozzarella bruschetta subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific bruschetta with mozzarella entry. 'Bread, toasted' (A0CJW) is the closest defensible parent category covering the toasted bread base.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for bruschetta alla mozzarella. No suitable parent concept identified without risk of fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is 6 pieces of mozzarella bruschetta, each piece approximately 40 g, totalling approximately 240 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bruschetta with cheese, composite estimate from toasted bread and mozzarella cheese components (SR Legacy: Bread, Italian, toasted; Cheese, mozzarella, whole milk)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for bruschetta alla mozzarella. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: Italian toasted bread (~60% by weight, FDC ID 172686) and whole-milk mozzarella cheese (~30% by weight, FDC ID 171242), with garlic butter (~10% by weight, estimated from butter with garlic). Per-100g values are derived from this composite blend. Energy cross-checked against published Italian food composition data (CREA, Banca Dati di Composizione degli Alimenti per Studi Epidemiologici in Italia).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white bread / Italian bread toasted analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care, 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bruschetta alla mozzarella. GI estimated at 70 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: toasted white/Italian bread, which has a published GI of approximately 70 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The mozzarella and garlic butter components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 68.4) / 100 = 47.9, where carbohydrate per 240 g serving = 28.5 × 2.4 = 68.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1152 mg per 6-piece serving) from garlic butter and mozzarella cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~1152 mg per serving, representing approximately 50% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-butter or reduced-cheese preparation if consumed occasionally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and high glycemic load per serving (~47.9) driven by refined white bread base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat this dish as a high-GI, high-GL food; the 6-piece serving delivers approximately 68 g of rapidly digestible carbohydrate, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion size should be strictly limited or the dish avoided in favour of lower-GI alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (~7.1 g per 100 g) from mozzarella cheese and garlic butter, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk should be aware that a 6-piece serving provides approximately 17 g of saturated fat and over 1100 mg of sodium, both of which can adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bruschetta alla Mozzarella is a classic Italian appetiser consisting of thick slices of bread — typically a rustic Italian loaf or baguette — spread with garlic butter and topped generously with mozzarella cheese, then oven-toasted until the cheese melts and the bread achieves a golden, crisp exterior. The dish is served in a set of six pieces, making it suitable as a shared starter or light snack. The texture contrasts a crunchy, buttery bread base with a soft, stretchy, milky mozzarella topping. The flavour profile is savoury and rich, with pronounced garlic and butter notes complemented by the mild, creamy character of whole-milk mozzarella. Nutritionally, each 100 g portion provides approximately 268 kcal, 10.2 g protein, 12.8 g fat (of which 7.1 g is saturated), and 28.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is a significant source of calcium (~185 mg per 100 g) from the mozzarella, but is also high in sodium (~480 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat. The glycemic index is estimated at 70 (high), driven by the refined white bread base, making the 6-piece serving a high glycemic load meal component (~47.9 GL). Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals with hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI and GL), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium). It is vegetarian but not vegan, contains gluten and dairy allergens, and is not certified halal or kosher. In Singapore, this dish is served at Italian restaurants such as Pasta Fresca Da Salvatore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for bruschetta alla mozzarella as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA SR Legacy composite of toasted Italian bread and whole-milk mozzarella cheese with garlic butter, cross-referenced against CREA Italian food composition data. GI is an analogous estimate based on the toasted white bread component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy component entries (toasted Italian bread ~60%, whole-milk mozzarella ~30%, garlic butter ~10% by weight). No single USDA or HPB entry covers this exact dish. 2. Serving size of 240 g (6 × 40 g pieces) is an estimate based on typical restaurant portion sizing for bruschetta; actual restaurant weight may vary. 3. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for toasted white/Italian bread; no direct GI measurement for bruschetta alla mozzarella was found in the approved literature. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9), trans_fat_g (0.1), potassium_mg (112.0), calcium_mg (185.0), iron_mg (1.4), cholesterol_mg (38.0), vitamin_c_mg (0.5), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (72.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.12), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.14), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.6), vitamin_b6_mg (0.05), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.4), folate_mcg_dfe (28.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.2), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (165.0), magnesium_mg (18.0), zinc_mg (0.9), selenium_mcg (12.0), water_g (44.0) are all estimated from the USDA SR Legacy composite and are not individually confirmed by a single source entry for this dish. 5. This is an Italian cuisine dish served in Singapore; multilingual naming rules for Italian cuisine do not mandate Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names. Chinese names are provided as optional additions for Singapore market context. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; garlic butter sourcing and cheese rennet type are unknown for the specific restaurant preparation.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from USDA SR Legacy component entries (toasted Italian bread, whole-milk mozzarella, garlic butter) as no single approved-source entry exists for this dish.
Applied GI analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for toasted white bread (GI 70) as no direct GI measurement exists for bruschetta alla mozzarella.
Used closest_parent match_type for FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology entries; SNOMED CT set to null with gap note to avoid fabrication.
Provided Chinese simplified and traditional names as supplementary Singapore-market aliases; confirmed Italian cuisine does not mandate Malay/Tamil/Hindi under G6 rules.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in aliases array.
Documented all estimated micronutrient values in notes with clear provenance statement.
Confirmed no core macro is zero; no zero-macro justification phrase required.
Confirmed all per_100g values are numeric; no -1 sentinel values used (all micronutrients estimated from composite, not missing).
